candidate_problems.text: What is the right formal criterion for a 'stably realizable' intelligence–goal bundle under reflection, self-modification, and environmental interaction? The conjecture hinges on a distinction between logical specifiability and dynamical stability, but leaves open how to define coherence, persistence, and attractor-status in a non-question-begging way. A new problem is to build a formal framework that distinguishes terminal goals from goal architectures and characterizes when an agent preserves, transforms, or functionally abandons its original objective over time.

How strong and universal are the proposed structural pressures across different environments, embodiments, and social worlds? The conjecture suggests convergent instrumental tendencies like self-preservation, resource acquisition, and optionality preservation, but it remains unresolved whether these are near-universal consequences of capability or artifacts of a particular class of worlds. A key open question is to map the boundary conditions under which orthogonality collapses dynamically versus those under which highly diverse goal systems remain stable.

Does selection on goal architectures induce any deeper convergence in values, or only convergence in methods? The conjecture claims advanced agents may converge in epistemic style and instrumental repertoire without converging in terminal aims, but this is an unresolved tension: reflective stability, ontology change, and long-horizon planning may transform terminal goals into more abstract or world-model-dependent forms. A worthwhile problem is to determine whether dynamical robustness merely filters for implementation features or also compresses the space of viable terminal values into a smaller equivalence class.
